sections.label: Exam at a glance
items: Proctored — online or Pearson VUE test center
Pass/fail + scaled score, plus percent-correct by domain

sections.label: How to use this guide
body: This guide goes beyond the official exam blueprint: each domain below adds reference-grounded detail drawn from Anthropic's live documentation, with links so you can read the source. Pair it with the practice questions — answers tagged “Docs” link to the exact Help Center page that backs them.

Fastest path to ready: skim the domain notes, take the free Anthropic Academy courses listed near the end (start with Claude 101 and AI Fluency), then drill with Practice and a full Mock exam. Aim for roughly 80% in practice before you sit the real thing — the 720 cut is about 69%, so give yourself margin.
Everything here is an independent study aid built from public documentation; it is not affiliated with or endorsed by Anthropic, and the sample questions are illustrative, not from the live exam.

sections.label: How scoring works
body: The exam is criterion-referenced: you're measured against a fixed standard set by subject-matter experts, not against other candidates. You pass by demonstrating the blueprint knowledge, not by beating a percentage of peers.

Your total scaled score (100–1,000) determines pass/fail; the cut is 720. The per-domain percent-correct on your report is diagnostic only — it shows where you were strong or weak but does not decide your result.

Practical takeaway: don't fixate on any single domain during the exam — every scored item counts toward the same total. In this app, the calibration bar estimates readiness from your practice accuracy; it's a study aid, not the official scaling.

sections.label: Domain 1 · Prompting and Task Execution
sections.overview: Write effective prompts, decompose complex requests, iterate to improve quality, and adapt your approach to the task type.
keyPoints: Be clear and direct: the docs say to treat Claude like a brilliant new colleague with no context — state the task, audience, format, tone, and length explicitly. Golden rule: if a colleague with minimal context would be confused by your prompt, so will Claude.
Give examples (multishot): one good input–output example steers format and style better than a pile of adjectives.
Assign a role/persona (in the prompt or Project instructions) to set the right perspective, vocabulary, and level of detail — it shapes tone, not factual accuracy.
Decompose and chain: break a complex goal into ordered steps you can check; ask Claude to reason step by step for multi-step problems.
Reduce hallucinations by explicitly allowing “I don't know” and asking Claude to flag uncertainty.
Prefer positive instructions (“use a professional, courteous tone”) over stacking “don't” rules; be specific about length and format (“5 bullets, each under 15 words”).
Don't over-engineer — longer prompts aren't automatically better; iterate on the first draft. Capture repeated work as a reusable template, and note that prompts largely carry across model upgrades.
Stuck starting? The Claude Console has a prompt generator to draft a first prompt.
sections.watchFor: The “best prompt” answer is almost always the most specific one that supplies context and constraints. Reusable templates beat re-writing instructions each time.

sections.label: Domain 2 · Output Evaluation and Validation
sections.overview: The largest domain. Evaluate outputs for accuracy and completeness, spot hallucinations, inconsistencies, and bias, validate claims, and decide when human review is required.
keyPoints: Verify before you trust: fluent, confident, well-structured output is not evidence of correctness — check specific claims, figures, and citations against authoritative sources.
Hallucination = confident, plausible-sounding content that is fabricated or unsupported (invented citations, quotes, statistics). Self-reported confidence and answer length are not accuracy signals.
The Research feature returns easy-to-check citations so you can trace each claim to its source — still verify anything high-stakes.
File-creation outputs (Excel formulas, reports, decks) are a strong starting point, but the docs say to review formulas, data, and sensitive content before sharing.
Match the need to the problem type: accuracy (wrong), completeness (missing required parts), consistency (self-contradiction), and bias (unfair assumptions) are distinct.
Human review scales with stakes: legal, medical, financial, external, or regulated outputs need verification regardless of polish.

Choose the output format for how it'll be consumed: an Artifact for substantial, reusable, editable deliverables (Claude makes one for self-contained content, typically over 15 lines); inline for quick replies; a downloadable file for polished .docx/.xlsx/.pptx/.pdf.
Curate rather than paste raw output: select, verify, and organize the relevant, accurate points.
sections.watchFor: When an answer sounds authoritative and cites something specific, that is exactly when to verify. Confidence is a style, not a fact-check.
refs: Create and edit files with Claude
Web search vs. extended thinking vs. research

sections.label: Domain 3 · Product and Model Selection
sections.overview: Select the right product feature and model tier, aligning to cost, speed, and quality, and manage context and memory.
keyPoints: Match model to task and cost: the usage-credits guidance says to use the efficient Haiku model or the most recent Sonnet for most (and high-volume) work, reserving Opus for genuinely complex reasoning. Your plan sets usage limits, not which model you can pick.

Projects are self-contained workspaces with shared instructions + a knowledge base; free users can create up to five, and paid plans scale large knowledge via RAG (up to ~10x) as it nears context limits.
Research (paid plans) runs multiple web + internal searches into a cited report and requires web search to be turned on.
Choose the retrieval tool: web search for current/changing facts; extended thinking for complex reasoning; Research for comprehensive, multi-source reports (roughly five or more tool calls).

Context is finite — distinguish usage limits (quantity over time; session limit resets every five hours) from length limits (one conversation). Hit a length limit → start a new chat or use a Project (RAG loads only relevant content).
Memory from chat history is available on all plans; searching past chats is paid-only and uses RAG; each Project has its own separate memory.
Code execution & file creation (all plans) produces real .xlsx/.docx/.pptx/.pdf files (30MB/file) in a sandbox, and also powers artifacts and Skills.
sections.watchFor: “Always use the top model” and “always use the cheapest” are both wrong. The right answer matches the tier to the task's real demands.

sections.label: Domain 4 · Workflow Integration and Solution Design
sections.overview: Analyze requirements and use cases, use Claude for research/planning/optimization, support design and iteration, integrate into workflows, and communicate value and limits to stakeholders.
keyPoints: Start from requirements and the use case: inputs, outputs, and success criteria — before choosing tools.
Map the current process; target high-value, lower-risk steps; preserve (or replace) controls that catch costly errors instead of automating them away.
Keep a human checkpoint before consequential actions. A concrete documented example: the Gmail connector lets Claude draft emails with context but cannot send them — a person sends the draft.
Use Research to gather multi-source, cited input for plans and proposals, and combine it with extended thinking to plan the approach and then execute.
Standardize recurring team deliverables with Skills so everyone gets consistent, expert-level results; ground a recurring initiative's context in a Project.
Communicate realistic benefits alongside limitations and required oversight; never promise zero errors or full replacement.
Know the associate scope boundary: escalate custom API integration and enterprise-scale agentic system design to Claude Architects/Developers.
sections.watchFor: Associate scope = translate business needs into effective Claude use and know when to escalate. Building against APIs or designing agentic systems is out of scope.

sections.label: Domain 5 · Configuration and Knowledge Management
sections.overview: Configure Projects with instructions and knowledge sources, manage connectors, write good system-level instructions, and keep configurations current.
keyPoints: Project instructions are inherited by every chat — keep them to role, general context, and key guidelines, and put task-specifics in the chat. The project name and description are informational and are not seen by Claude.
Project knowledge grounds responses in your team's current, approved documents; update or replace files when policy changes, and remove unused files to preserve context.

Connectors (Gmail, Calendar, Drive) are available to all users; on Team/Enterprise an Owner or Primary Owner must enable them org-wide first. Claude mirrors your existing permissions, pulls only what you ask for, and reads Drive files as text content only (no images/comments).
Enable Code execution & file creation in Settings > Capabilities (Free/Pro/Max) or Organization settings (Team/Enterprise) — it also powers artifacts and Skills.

Skills are folders with a SKILL.md that load on demand via progressive disclosure (keeping context lean); built-in Office/PDF skills auto-invoke, org owners can provision skills, and custom skills are private unless shared.
For code-execution security, network egress can be off, package-managers-only, or allowlisted domains — “off” is the most secure baseline (new Enterprise orgs default to off).
sections.watchFor: When a Project misbehaves, check the instructions first — is the rule stated clearly and not contradicted?

sections.label: Domain 6 · Governance, Risk, and Responsible Use
sections.overview: Identify appropriate vs. inappropriate use cases, apply data-sensitivity/privacy/regulatory considerations, follow organizational AI policy, and weigh ethical implications.
keyPoints: Handle sensitive data by policy: anonymize or redact regulated identifiers (PII, health, financial) and third-party-confidential information before use; data classified “restricted” → decline or escalate.

Consumer training is opt-in: the “Help improve Claude” toggle in Privacy Settings controls whether your chats train future models, and turning it off is forward-looking (it can't pull back data already in a training run). Flagged conversations may still be used for safety even if you opt out.

Team, Enterprise, and API data isn't used for training by default; Gmail/Drive/Calendar connector data isn't used for training unless you copy it into the chat; incognito chats are never used for training.
Thumbs up/down feedback stores the entire related conversation for up to five years.

Incognito chat (ghost icon, outside projects) isn't saved to history or memory; on Team/Enterprise it's retained at least 30 days for safety and appears in organizational exports and the Compliance API.

Keep a human accountable for decisions about people and watch for bias; don't present unverified output as authoritative legal, medical, or tax advice; be appropriately transparent about AI use; follow the org's AI policy and escalate when a use case is unclear.
Code execution can give Claude limited internet access — monitor chats with sensitive data, or disable network egress to prevent data leaving the sandbox.
sections.watchFor: The safe answer usually enables the task responsibly (anonymize, get clearance, escalate) rather than either uploading as-is or abandoning it entirely.
refs: Is my data used for model training?

sections.label: Domain 7 · Troubleshooting and Optimization
sections.overview: Diagnose and fix underperforming prompts and poor outputs, adjust based on feedback, and optimize workflows for efficiency and effectiveness.
keyPoints: Off-target output? Diagnose the prompt first: clarify the goal, add missing context, specify format, give an example, or decompose the task — before switching tools.
Dropped sections → enumerate every required part or split into steps. A format that's sometimes ignored → confirm the rule is stated clearly and not contradicted elsewhere.
Long or drifting conversation → summarize the essentials and restart, or move the work into a Project (RAG loads only relevant context). A length limit → new chat or projects.
Research not digging in? Confirm web search is on and Research is active — or prompt “Claude, please use the research tool to…”
No files or artifacts appearing? Enable Code execution & file creation in Settings > Capabilities.
Google connector erroring (“you may need to reconnect”) → Customize > Connectors, disconnect, then re-authenticate on next use.

Too slow or costly at volume → route routine, high-volume steps to Haiku or the most recent Sonnet and reserve Opus for the hard steps. Optimize with evidence, one variable at a time, and capture what works as a reusable template or Skill.
sections.watchFor: Repeating the identical prompt or declaring the task impossible are never the right troubleshooting answer.

sections.label: Claude feature reference (from the docs)
sections.intro: Verified facts for the features the prep section names. The Docs-tagged practice questions are written against these pages.

facts: Self-contained workspaces with their own chat history and knowledge base; available on all plans (free users: up to five projects).
Project instructions are inherited by every chat in the project; the name and description are informational and not seen by Claude.
Knowledge-base files are available across all chats in the project; on paid plans, enhanced project knowledge scales large content via RAG (up to ~10x).
Each project has its own scoped memory; contexts don't bleed across projects or into global memory. Team/Enterprise can share projects (can-use vs. can-edit).
Created when content is significant, self-contained (typically over 15 lines), and likely to be edited, reused, or referred back to outside the chat.
Require Code execution and file creation to be enabled — the same capability that powers Word/Excel/PowerPoint/PDF creation.
Render in a side window and support a version selector; Markdown supports in-place ‘Edit with Claude’; your edits don't change Claude's memory of the original.
AI-powered artifacts run on each user's own subscription (no API keys, free to share) — don't publish confidential information.
Available to all plans (Free–Enterprise); creates .xlsx, .pptx, .docx, and .pdf files you can download or save to Google Drive.
Runs code in a private, sandboxed computing environment; maximum file size is 30MB per file (uploads and downloads).
Enabled in Settings > Capabilities (Free/Pro/Max) or Organization settings (Team/Enterprise). New Enterprise orgs default to network egress off.
Network egress options: off (most secure), package-managers-only, or allowlisted domains. Review generated formulas, data, and sensitive content before sharing.
Memory from chat history is available on all plans; searching past chats is paid-only, uses RAG, and shows as a tool call. The memory summary refreshes about every 24 hours.
Global memory synthesis excludes chats inside projects; deleting a conversation removes it from memory.
Incognito chat (ghost icon, outside projects only) isn't saved to history or memory and can't be reopened.
On Team/Enterprise, incognito chats are retained at least 30 days for safety and appear in organizational exports and the Compliance API.
Folders with a SKILL.md, loaded on demand via progressive disclosure (keeps context lean); require code execution.
Built-in Anthropic skills (Excel, Word, PowerPoint, PDF) are invoked automatically when relevant; custom skills are private unless shared.
A skill's description determines when it activates — a vague description is why a skill ‘won't fire’. Org owners can provision skills org-wide.
MCP provides access to external tools/data; Skills provide the procedure for a task. Custom instructions are broad; Skills are task-specific.
Research (paid plans) runs multiple, building searches across the web and your connected Gmail/Calendar/Docs, producing a longer cited report; it requires web search to be on.
Use web search for current/changing facts; extended thinking for complex reasoning; Research for multi-source reports (~5+ tool calls).
Research runs agentically and can work in the background; if it answers shallowly, prompt it to ‘use the research tool’.
Research draws on the same usage limits and can consume them faster.
Gmail, Calendar, and Drive connectors are available to all users; on Team/Enterprise an Owner must enable them org-wide first.
Claude mirrors your existing permissions, accesses data only when you ask, and reads Drive files as text content only (no images/comments).
Gmail: search, read, and draft — but Claude cannot send email. Every action requires your approval.
Anthropic doesn't train on connector data (unless you copy it into the chat); retrieved data is stored with the chat and deleted when you delete the chat.
Pro is $20/month with at least 5x the free per-session usage; the session limit resets every five hours, with a separate weekly limit.
Team supports up to 150 seats (minimum five); Standard seats get 1.25x Pro per-session usage, Premium seats 6.25x; limits are per-member, not pooled.
Team includes centralized admin/billing, SSO and role-based access, and workplace connectors; go to Enterprise beyond 150 seats.
Usage limits = quantity over time; length limits = how long one conversation can get. Paid plans can enable usage credits to continue at standard API rates.

sections.label: Training courses (Anthropic Academy)
sections.intro: Anthropic's official courses are free and hosted on Skilljar (Anthropic Academy). For CCAO-F, take them roughly in this order — Claude 101 and AI Fluency cover the most exam ground.
items: Start here. Everyday work tasks and core features (chats, file uploads, Projects) — foundational for every domain.
Collaborate with AI effectively, efficiently, ethically, and safely — maps to D1 (prompting), D2 (evaluation), and D6 (responsible use).
How AI works and where it fails — supports D2 (hallucinations/validation) and D6.
Task loop, file and research workflows, plugins and skills — supports D3/D4/D5.
Build, configure, and share Skills — supports D5 (configuration & knowledge management).
Full catalog on Skilljar (also API, MCP, and Claude Code courses for deeper/adjacent topics).
Landing page for Anthropic's learning resources and course paths.
Official exam page (Anthropic Partner Network / Partner Academy) — exam guide, policies, and registration.

sections.label: Test-taking strategy
body: Read the stem for the decision it asks for — usually ‘the most appropriate action.’ Many options are true-ish; pick the one that best fits responsible, effective practice.

Watch for absolute distractors: ‘always,’ ‘never,’ ‘send as-is,’ ‘no review.’ They're usually wrong. Balanced answers that verify, anonymize, escalate, or match the tool to the task are usually right.
Multiple-response items tell you how many to select — select exactly that many.
Eliminate the two clearly-wrong options first, then choose between the remaining two on which is safer and more effective.
With 60 items in 120 minutes you have ~2 minutes each — flag hard ones and move on, then return.

sections.label: Exam-day logistics
body: Register through the Anthropic Partner Network / Partner Academy; the exam is delivered by Pearson VUE (online-proctored or test center).
Bring valid, unexpired, government-issued photo ID whose name matches your registration exactly.
You may cancel or reschedule up to 24 hours before; changes inside 24 hours forfeit the fee.
Keep your workspace clear — no phones, smart watches, headphones, notes, or secondary monitors. You must stay in view of the webcam if testing online.
Retakes: 14 days after a first fail, 30 after a second, 90 after a third; up to four attempts per rolling 12 months, fee each time.
You accept a confidentiality/NDA before starting; exam content is confidential and must not be disclosed.
